metadata.title: Michigan Tax Policy Tracker
metadata.headline: Select a tax area to view related bills, fiscal-note priorities, and the policy landscape.
metadata.disclaimer: Personal public-source tracker. Not an official publication or position of the Michigan Department of Treasury or State of Michigan.

tracker_areas.label: Crypto & digital assets
tracker_areas.headline: No tracked bill is enacted. One has passed the House; two are on House third reading.
tracker_areas.landscape_description: The 22 tracked bills organized by what they govern and what they can teach us about potential tax design.
tracker_areas.monitoring_rule: Every crypto bill is reviewed for potential tax-design lessons. A bill enters the fiscal-note queue only when an official committee hearing is scheduled.

policy_landscape.current_picture: Pending proposals address tax neutrality for digital-asset payments, a mining-income deduction, and a sub-$200 transaction and reporting exemption.
policy_landscape.status: HB 4511 is on House third reading; HB 4513 and HB 5132 remain in House committees.
policy_landscape.tax_design_lens: Define whether the policy is neutral treatment, a targeted preference, or de minimis relief, then specify valuation, attribution, aggregation, and reporting rules.

policy_landscape.current_picture: Proposals cover a state-backed digital currency, crypto-paid taxes and fees, state and retirement-fund investment, and a digital-currency wage option.
policy_landscape.status: HB 4510 is on House third reading; the other three remain in House committees.
policy_landscape.tax_design_lens: Resolve asset eligibility, valuation time, conversion, custody, withholding, settlement, and allocation of receipts among state funds.

policy_landscape.current_picture: The bills address industrial zoning, abandoned-well mining, large-facility registration and financial security, and limits on local moratoria.
policy_landscape.status: All four remain in House committees.
policy_landscape.tax_design_lens: Facility thresholds, qualifying activity, nexus, incentives, registration fees, environmental obligations, and state-local authority would shape any mining levy.

policy_landscape.current_picture: Competing modernization bills define when virtual-currency exchange, transfer, or custody falls inside or outside money-transmitter regulation.
policy_landscape.status: HB 5544 passed the House and is in a Senate committee; SB 835 remains in a Senate committee.
policy_landscape.tax_design_lens: A tax registration or third-party reporting system cannot assume every relevant crypto intermediary is licensed as a money transmitter.

policy_landscape.area: Retail crypto ATMs and consumer protection
policy_landscape.current_picture: Michigan proposals range from licensing and anti-fraud controls to civil or felony bans, with the newest package using transaction caps, holds, warnings, receipts, and records.
policy_landscape.status: All nine remain in House or Senate committees.
policy_landscape.tax_design_lens: Operator and premises responsibility, transaction records, thresholds, aggregation, and enforcement are useful design inputs; fines are not a dependable tax base.

bills.policy: Makes digital-asset mining over 1 MW a permitted industrial-zone use and bars mining-specific special-use, zoning, and noise restrictions not applied to comparable data centers.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Provides a statutory mining threshold and comparison to data centers that could inform the taxable activity, facility classification, nexus, and local-preemption rules of a future mining tax.
bills.tax_fiscal: HFA: no direct state or local fiscal impact; local zoning authority is preempted.
bills.latest_action: Re-referred to House Communications and Technology after placement on second reading.
bills.next_trigger: Committee hearing, substitute, or report to the House floor.

bills.policy: Creates a Treasury bullion depository and Michcoin, a state-issued gold- and silver-backed digital currency with wallets, transfer, redemption, fees, bonds, and trust custody.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Illustrates payment, wallet, custody, redemption, fee, and reserve mechanics that would matter if Michigan accepted a digital asset for tax payment or needed to value and settle digital receipts.
bills.tax_fiscal: Potential Treasury startup, custody, bond, and operating exposure; fees are authorized; no public fiscal analysis located.
bills.fiscal_channel: Treasury startup, fees, bonds, and custody
bills.latest_action: Re-referred to House Communications and Technology after placement on second reading.
bills.next_trigger: Committee hearing, fiscal analysis, or substitute addressing Treasury capitalization and custody.

bills.policy: Allows the Treasurer to invest up to 10% of both the General Fund and Budget Stabilization Fund in cryptocurrency, establish custody, lend holdings, and process crypto-paid taxes or fees.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Directly tests how crypto-paid taxes and fees would be valued, converted, held, and allocated among funds, making it a core administrative model for any future crypto tax-payment policy.
bills.tax_fiscal: HFA: no direct impact, but creates market exposure for GF and BSF balances; crypto receipts would be moved to GF with other funds reimbursed in dollars.
bills.fiscal_channel: Tax receipts and state fund assets
bills.latest_action: Re-referred to House Communications and Technology after placement on second reading.
bills.next_trigger: Committee action or amendment to the 10% cap, eligible funds, custody, or lending authority.
important_notes: The authority is permissive, not mandatory, and the 10% cap applies separately to available General Fund and Budget Stabilization Fund balances.
Crypto paid as taxes or fees would be transferred to the General Fund, with any legally designated fund reimbursed in dollars.
Holdings could be kept through state custody, a qualified custodian, or a registered exchange-traded product, and could be lent under Treasurer rules.
ambiguities: The bill does not define available funds or specify valuation and rebalancing timing for the 10% limits.
The conversion date and exchange rate used to reimburse a designated fund after a crypto payment are not specified.
The standard allowing lending without increasing financial risk has no statutory collateral, counterparty, concentration, or loss-allocation rule.

bills.policy: Allows up to 5% of State Investment Board-managed retirement assets in cryptocurrency averaging at least $100 billion market capitalization; direct holdings must use an approved exchange-traded product.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Shows one administrable approach to eligible-asset definitions, market-cap thresholds, valuation, and custody, although those investment rules would not themselves determine a tax base.
bills.tax_fiscal: HFA: no direct impact; pension and OPEB effects depend on investment decisions and returns.
bills.latest_action: H-2 substitute adopted and amended; placed on third reading. No House passage is shown.
bills.next_trigger: House passage, floor amendment, or removal from the calendar.

bills.policy: Preempts bans, licensing, and extra taxes based solely on digital-asset use; protects nodes, transfers, and staking; restricts state CBDC advocacy and certain validator liability.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Draws a central tax-neutrality boundary: ordinary taxes on the underlying transaction remain possible, but an additional charge imposed solely because digital assets are used would be barred.
bills.tax_fiscal: HFA: no direct impact. Crypto-only taxes or charges are barred, while ordinary taxes on the underlying transaction remain allowed.
bills.latest_action: H-1 substitute adopted; placed on third reading. No House passage is shown.
bills.next_trigger: House passage or amendment to tax preemption, staking, CBDC, or liability provisions.
important_notes: The bill does not exempt an underlying sale or transfer from ordinary tax; it bars an additional tax, withholding, assessment, or charge imposed solely because digital assets are used.
It preempts state and local restrictions on holding digital assets, operating nodes, transferring assets, and participating in staking.
It also restricts state-agency support for a federal central bank digital currency and creates a civil-liability protection for specified network activities.
ambiguities: The boundary between a prohibited crypto-only charge and a generally applicable licensing, compliance, or service fee is not specified.
The civil-liability protection may be unclear for mixed-service providers or claims not based solely on validating a transaction.
The scope of advocating for or supporting federal central bank digital currency testing or adoption is undefined.

bills.policy: Creates an EGLE program allowing selected firms to use gas from abandoned wells to power bitcoin mining in exchange for plugging, response activity, and site restoration obligations.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Defines a qualifying mining activity, site, operator, and verification program that could inform eligibility and substantiation rules for a mining tax, credit, deduction, or environmental offset.
bills.tax_fiscal: HFA: indeterminate Orphan Well Fund costs and revenues; the fund could support startup costs and administration.
bills.fiscal_channel: Orphan Well Fund and program administration
bills.latest_action: Re-referred to House Communications and Technology after placement on second reading.
bills.next_trigger: Committee action, program funding changes, or movement of the tie-barred tax deduction.

bills.policy: Deducts income attributable to bitcoin mining under HB 4512's abandoned-well program from IIT, CIT, and flow-through entity business income tax bases.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Directly tests tax-base attribution across IIT, CIT, and flow-through entities, including eligibility verification, mixed-activity allocation, revenue-loss measurement, and incentive additionality.
bills.tax_fiscal: Direct tax-base reduction. HFA: indeterminate revenue loss depending on whether subsidized mining would occur without the deduction.
bills.fiscal_channel: IIT, CIT, and flow-through entity tax bases
bills.latest_action: Re-referred to House Communications and Technology after placement on second reading.
bills.next_trigger: Committee action or amendment to eligible income, taxes, duration, or tie-bar.
important_notes: The deduction applies across the individual income tax, corporate income tax, and elective flow-through entity business income tax.
Eligibility is limited to bitcoin mining under the abandoned-well program created by tie-barred HB 4512.
HFA identifies an indeterminate revenue loss that depends heavily on whether the mining activity would occur without the deduction.
ambiguities: The bill provides no allocation method for income attributable to qualifying mining when a taxpayer has mixed sites or activities.
The introduced language does not specify a first eligible tax year, sunset, or statewide cap.
Treatment of related expenses, losses, asset-sale timing, and pass-through member allocation is not specifically addressed.

bills.policy: Protects lawful convertible-virtual-currency use, self-hosted wallets, and collateral; bars state or local tax and tax reporting on covered transactions below $200 and authorizes treble damages.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Provides a concrete de minimis model for small transactions and exposes the key design questions for valuation timing, aggregation, wallet neutrality, reporting relief, and interaction with income or gain taxes.
bills.tax_fiscal: Direct de minimis tax and reporting exemption below $200, plus governmental litigation exposure; no public fiscal analysis located.
bills.fiscal_channel: De minimis tax and reporting exemption
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Finance.
bills.next_trigger: Finance hearing or amendment to the $200 threshold, covered taxes, reporting, or treble damages.
important_notes: The tax and tax-reporting prohibition applies to covered transactions below $200 and reaches state and local governmental entities.
The bill separately protects lawful use of convertible virtual currency, self-hosted wallets, and use of virtual currency as collateral.
A prevailing plaintiff would be entitled to three times actual damages, creating potential governmental litigation exposure.
ambiguities: Transaction is undefined, leaving the valuation time, aggregation of related transfers, and treatment of multiple sub-$200 payments unclear.
The phrase impose a tax on a transaction does not identify which state or local taxes are displaced or how income and gain taxes would be treated.
The measure of actual loss for treble damages and the provision's interaction with governmental immunity are not specified.

bills.policy: Regulates virtual-currency kiosks through disclosures, warnings, receipts, blockchain analytics, antifraud and due-diligence policies, compliance officers, quarterly DIFS reporting, and local preemption.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Its customer, transaction, receipt, blockchain-analytics, and operator-reporting requirements show what third-party records might support registration or audit, though regulatory records would not automatically become tax data.
bills.tax_fiscal: Indeterminate DIFS, court, and civil-fine effects; up to $1,000 per knowing and willful violation; no tax-base change.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Regulatory Reform.
bills.next_trigger: Committee action or consolidation with later kiosk regulation and ban packages.

bills.policy: Treats owning, operating, facilitating, marketing, or advertising a virtual-currency kiosk as licensed money transmission and subjects operators to existing bonding requirements.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Licensing and bonding could identify responsible kiosk operators and collection intermediaries, helping define registration, nexus, and enforcement responsibility under a transaction-based tax.
bills.tax_fiscal: Licensing and surety-bond coverage; no direct tax-base change identified.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Regulatory Reform.
bills.next_trigger: Committee action or interaction with the HB 5544 money-transmitter rewrite.

bills.policy: Replaces Michigan's money-transmission act with the CSBS model framework effective July 1, 2027. Pure virtual-currency exchange, transfer, or custody is excluded when no legal-tender transfer is involved.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Clarifies which virtual-currency businesses fall outside money-transmitter licensing, warning that a future tax registration or reporting system could not rely on money-transmitter status alone.
bills.tax_fiscal: HFA: likely minimal one-time DIFS cost; licensing, penalties, and criminal-justice effects are indeterminate. This is regulation, not a crypto tax.
bills.latest_action: Referred to Senate Finance, Insurance, and Consumer Protection after House passage 97-10 on June 25.
bills.next_trigger: Senate committee hearing, substitute, report, or substitution for SB 835.

bills.policy: Senate version of a Money Transmission Modernization Act that replaces the current act and expressly addresses when virtual-currency activity falls outside money transmission.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Offers a second legislative definition of regulated and excluded virtual-currency activity, useful for checking whether future tax registration or third-party reporting would leave coverage gaps.
bills.tax_fiscal: Regulatory and licensing effects; no public fiscal analysis located. Compare against House-passed HB 5544 before relying on details.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to Senate Finance, Insurance, and Consumer Protection.
bills.next_trigger: Committee substitution, merger with HB 5544, or Senate report.

bills.policy: Beginning January 1, 2027, requires a digital-currency wage option for classified salaried state employees, with at least six choices including Bitcoin and excluding a government-controlled digital currency.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Surfaces payroll-tax design issues: when wages are valued, who converts and custodies the asset, how withholding is remitted in dollars, and how gains or losses after conversion are separated from wage income.
bills.tax_fiscal: Potential payroll, vendor, custody, conversion, withholding, and administrative costs; no public fiscal analysis located.
bills.fiscal_channel: State payroll, conversion, and withholding administration
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Government Operations.
bills.next_trigger: Committee hearing, fiscal analysis, or amendment addressing conversion timing and tax withholding.

bills.policy: Creates registration, financial-security, decommissioning, and reporting requirements for large-scale data centers, expressly including qualifying cryptocurrency-mining facilities.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Provides facility thresholds, registration, fees, reporting, financial security, and a dedicated-fund model that could inform the administration and earmarking of a mining or data-center levy.
bills.tax_fiscal: Registration fees of $25,000-$100,000, reimbursement and financial-security requirements, and a dedicated fund; no public fiscal analysis located.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Government Operations.
bills.next_trigger: Committee action or changes to thresholds, fees, financial security, or mining coverage.

bills.policy: Regulates local moratoria on large data centers and cryptocurrency-mining facilities: initial moratoriums are capped at six months with one extension up to 90 days and specified public-process requirements.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Shows how mining facilities may be defined and locally preempted, which matters for assigning state versus local taxing authority and avoiding inconsistent local classifications.
bills.tax_fiscal: Potential local planning and study costs; no direct tax-base change identified.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Government Operations.
bills.next_trigger: Committee action or amendment to moratorium duration, disclosure, or local authority.

bills.policy: Prohibits installing, operating, or allowing a cryptocurrency kiosk on controlled premises.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Identifies kiosk operators and premise controllers as separate responsible parties, a useful enforcement distinction if a future tax or reporting duty is imposed on operators, retailers, or both.
bills.tax_fiscal: Civil fine up to $100 per day deposited in GF; possible prosecutor and Attorney General costs; no tax-base change.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Regulatory Reform.
bills.next_trigger: Committee action or consolidation with other kiosk-ban and regulation bills.

bills.policy: Allows the Lottery Commissioner to suspend a lottery sales-agent license for violation of HB 5986's cryptocurrency-kiosk prohibition.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Shows a cross-license enforcement mechanism through the lottery retailer network; it may help compliance but does not define a crypto tax base or collection method.
bills.tax_fiscal: Potential lottery license and retailer effects; no direct tax-base change identified.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Regulatory Reform.
bills.next_trigger: Movement of HB 5986 or changes to lottery-license sanctions.

bills.policy: Prohibits installing, operating, or allowing a cryptocurrency kiosk on controlled premises, using state civil infractions rather than criminal penalties.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Provides an alternative civil-enforcement structure and premise-responsibility rule, useful for comparing administrability and deterrence but not a stable revenue mechanism.
bills.tax_fiscal: Civil fines of $2,000-$5,000 for a first violation and $5,000-$10,000 for later violations; no tax-base change.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to Senate Finance, Insurance, and Consumer Protection.
bills.next_trigger: Committee hearing or selection of this bill over competing House kiosk approaches.

bills.policy: Prohibits operating or allowing a virtual-currency kiosk and makes violations felonies, with enhanced penalties for vulnerable-adult transactions, losses above $50,000, or organized fraud.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Its transaction-based penalties, forfeiture rules, loss thresholds, and operator definitions provide enforcement concepts, but fines and forfeitures should not be treated as a dependable tax base.
bills.tax_fiscal: Criminal-justice and enforcement costs; fines up to $50,000 or $100,000, transaction-based multiples, forfeiture, and injunctions; no tax-base change.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Regulatory Reform.
bills.next_trigger: Committee action or reconciliation with civil-ban and regulatory kiosk packages.

bills.policy: Adds HB 6077's virtual-currency-kiosk offenses to Michigan's sentencing guidelines.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Has limited independent tax-design value; it distinguishes criminal sentencing consequences from the civil assessment and administrative remedies normally used to enforce taxes.
bills.tax_fiscal: Sentencing companion with possible corrections and court costs; no tax-base change.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Regulatory Reform.
bills.next_trigger: Movement or amendment of HB 6077.

bills.policy: Regulates rather than bans physical crypto kiosks with a $500 daily per-operator cap, 24-hour new-user hold, secure locations, scam warnings, receipts, records, and Attorney General enforcement.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Creates transaction definitions, a per-operator daily threshold, receipts, records, and operator duties that could inform a kiosk tax or reporting regime, especially aggregation and responsible-party rules.
bills.tax_fiscal: Indeterminate positive GF fines up to $10,000 or $25,000 per violating transaction and possible Attorney General costs; no tax-base change.
bills.fiscal_channel: GF fines and Attorney General enforcement
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Regulatory Reform. A future-dated July 29 reproduction line is not treated as a substantive action.
bills.next_trigger: Committee hearing, substitute, HFA analysis, or consolidation with the kiosk-ban bills.

bills.policy: Makes a violation of HB 6222's cryptocurrency-exchange-kiosk regulation act an unlawful trade practice under the Michigan Consumer Protection Act.
bills.tax_policy_signal: Shows how existing consumer-protection enforcement and private remedies can supplement operator regulation; those remedies are distinct from tax assessment, collection, and revenue allocation.
bills.tax_fiscal: Consumer-protection enforcement and private-remedy implications; no tax-base change.
bills.latest_action: Introduced and referred to House Regulatory Reform. A future-dated July 29 reproduction line is not treated as a substantive action.
bills.next_trigger: Movement or amendment of HB 6222 and clarification of MCPA remedies.

key_updates.update: New targeted crypto-ATM regulation package introduced; committee only, not a tax and not enacted.
Sent to Senate committee after House passage; this is the only core tracked bill to pass either chamber.
Felony crypto-ATM ban package introduced and referred to House Regulatory Reform.
Civil crypto-ATM ban alternatives introduced in both chambers.
Moved to House third reading; no later House passage is shown in official histories.
